epigraph.text: For nothing is hidden that will not be made manifest, nor is anything secret that will not be known.
epigraph.quote: In complex analysis, sine and cosine reveal their true nature: they are exponentials in disguise.

content.text:
Euler's formula $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$ suggests natural 
extensions of trigonometric functions to complex arguments.

These definitions agree with the real functions when $z$ is real (by 
Euler's formula) and extend them analytically to all of $\C$.

content.title: Properties of Complex Sine and Cosine
content.content:
1. $\cos z$ and $\sin z$ are entire functions.

2. $(\cos z)' = -\sin z$ and $(\sin z)' = \cos z$.

3. $\cos^2 z + \sin^2 z = 1$.

4. $\cos(-z) = \cos z$ (even) and $\sin(-z) = -\sin z$ (odd).

5. $\cos(z + 2\pi) = \cos z$ and $\sin(z + 2\pi) = \sin z$.

6. $\sin z = 0$ if and only if $z = n\pi$ for integer $n$.

7. $\cos z = 0$ if and only if $z = (n + 1/2)\pi$ for integer $n$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: $|\sin z|$ and $|\cos z|$ are unbounded
content.content:
Unlike the real case, $|\sin z|$ and $|\cos z|$ can be arbitrarily large 
for complex $z$. For example, $|\sin(iy)| = |\sinh y| \to \infty$ as 
$|y| \to \infty$.

content.title: Real and Imaginary Parts
content.content:
For $z = x + iy$:
$$
 \cos(x + iy) &= \cos x \cosh y - i\sin x \sinh y \\
 \sin(x + iy) &= \sin x \cosh y + i\cos x \sinh y
$$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For cosine:
$$
 \cos(x + iy) &= \frac{e^{i(x+iy)} + e^{-i(x+iy)}}{2} 
 = \frac{e^{ix - y} + e^{-ix + y}}{2} \\
 &= \frac{e^{-y}(\cos x + i\sin x) + e^{y}(\cos x - i\sin x)}{2} \\
 &= \cos x \cdot \frac{e^y + e^{-y}}{2} - i\sin x \cdot \frac{e^y - e^{-y}}{2} \\
 &= \cos x \cosh y - i\sin x \sinh y.
$$

content.title: Relations Between Trig and Hyperbolic
content.content:
$$
 \cos(iz) &= \cosh z, & \sin(iz) &= i\sinh z, \\
 \cosh(iz) &= \cos z, & \sinh(iz) &= i\sin z.
$$
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
These relations show that trigonometric and hyperbolic functions are 
intimately connected through multiplication by $i$. In the complex domain, 
they are essentially the same functions rotated by $90Â°$.

The inverse functions $\arcsin z$, $\arccos z$, etc., can be defined 
using the logarithm.

content.content:
Let $w = \arcsin z$, so $\sin w = z$. Then:
\[
 z = \frac{e^{iw} - e^{-iw}}{2i}.
\]
Setting $\zeta = e^{iw}$, this becomes $2iz = \zeta - 1/\zeta$, or 
$\zeta^2 - 2iz\zeta - 1 = 0$. By the quadratic formula:
\[
 \zeta = iz + \sqrt{1 - z^2}.
\]
Thus $e^{iw} = iz + \sqrt{1 - z^2}$, and $iw = \log(iz + \sqrt{1 - z^2})$.

content.text: Since both $\sqrt{1 - z^2}$ and $\log$ are multi-valued, so is $\arcsin z$.

marginNotes.text:
Their voice goes out through all the earth, 
and their words to the end of the world. In them he has set a tent for the 
sun.

marginNotes.author: Roger Cotes (1714)
marginNotes.text:
The logarithm of a negative number is 
an imaginary quantity.
